title: Mesolithic

infobox.value: ([Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): μέσος, *mesos* 'middle' + λίθος, *lithos* 'stone') is the [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World") [archaeological period](/wiki/Archaeological_period "Archaeological period") between the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") and the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic"). The term [Epipaleolithic](/wiki/Epipaleolithic "Epipaleolithic") is often used synonymously, especially for outside northern Europe, and for the corresponding period in [the Levant](/wiki/Epipaleolithic_Near_East "Epipaleolithic Near East") and [Caucasus](/wiki/Epipaleolithic_Caucasus "Epipaleolithic Caucasus"). The Mesolithic has different time spans in different parts of [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia"). It refers to the final period of [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") cultures in Europe and the Middle East, between the end of the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum") and the [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution"). In Europe it spans roughly 15,000 to 5,000 [BP](/wiki/Before_Present "Before Present"); in the Middle East (the [Epipalaeolithic Near East](/wiki/Epipalaeolithic_Near_East "Epipalaeolithic Near East")) roughly 20,000 to 10,000 [BP](/wiki/Before_Present "Before Present"). The term is less used of areas farther east, and not at all of those beyond [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia") and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa").

The **Mesolithic** ([Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): μέσος, *mesos* 'middle' + λίθος, *lithos* 'stone') is the [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World") [archaeological period](/wiki/Archaeological_period "Archaeological period") between the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") and the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic"). The term [Epipaleolithic](/wiki/Epipaleolithic "Epipaleolithic") is often used synonymously, especially for outside northern Europe, and for the corresponding period in [the Levant](/wiki/Epipaleolithic_Near_East "Epipaleolithic Near East") and [Caucasus](/wiki/Epipaleolithic_Caucasus "Epipaleolithic Caucasus"). The Mesolithic has different time spans in different parts of [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia"). It refers to the final period of [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") cultures in Europe and the Middle East, between the end of the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum") and the [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution"). In Europe it spans roughly 15,000 to 5,000 [BP](/wiki/Before_Present "Before Present"); in the Middle East (the [Epipalaeolithic Near East](/wiki/Epipalaeolithic_Near_East "Epipalaeolithic Near East")) roughly 20,000 to 10,000 [BP](/wiki/Before_Present "Before Present"). The term is less used of areas farther east, and not at all of those beyond [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia") and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa").

The type of culture associated with the Mesolithic varies between areas, but it is associated with a decline in the group hunting of large animals in favour of a broader [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") way of life, and the development of more sophisticated and typically smaller lithic tools and weapons than the heavy-chipped equivalents typical of the Paleolithic. Depending on the region, some use of [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery") and [textiles](/wiki/History_of_clothing_and_textiles#Prehistoric_development "History of clothing and textiles") may be found in sites allocated to the Mesolithic, but generally indications of agriculture are taken as marking [transition into the Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution"). The more permanent settlements tend to be close to the sea or inland waters offering a good supply of food. Mesolithic societies are not seen as very complex, and burials are fairly simple; in contrast, grandiose [burial mounds](/wiki/Tumulus "Tumulus") are a mark of the Neolithic.

## Terminology

Main article: [Three-age system § Stone Age subdivisions](/wiki/Three-age_system#Stone_Age_subdivisions "Three-age system")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Evolution_of_temperature_in_the_Post-Glacial_period_according_to_Greenland_ice_cores.jpg/500px-Evolution_of_temperature_in_the_Post-Glacial_period_according_to_Greenland_ice_cores.jpg)](/wiki/File:Evolution_of_temperature_in_the_Post-Glacial_period_according_to_Greenland_ice_cores.jpg)

The Mesolithic begins during the latest [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene"), characterized by a progressive rise of temperatures, between the end of the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum") and the [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution") during the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene"). Evolution of temperature in the Post-Glacial period according to [Greenland ice cores](/wiki/Greenland_ice_cores "Greenland ice cores").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/OpgravingStevoort.jpg/250px-OpgravingStevoort.jpg)](/wiki/File:OpgravingStevoort.jpg)

Mesolithic artifacts

The terms "Paleolithic" and "Neolithic" were introduced by [John Lubbock](/wiki/John_Lubbock,_1st_Baron_Avebury "John Lubbock, 1st Baron Avebury") in his work *Pre-historic Times* in 1865. The additional "Mesolithic" category was added as an intermediate category by Hodder Westropp in 1866. Westropp's suggestion was immediately controversial. A British school led by [John Evans](/wiki/John_Evans_(archaeologist) "John Evans (archaeologist)") denied any need for an intermediate: the ages blended together like the colors of a rainbow, he said. A European school led by [Gabriel de Mortillet](/wiki/Gabriel_de_Mortillet "Gabriel de Mortillet") asserted that there was a gap between the earlier and later.

[Edouard Piette](/wiki/Edouard_Piette "Edouard Piette") claimed to have filled the gap with his naming of the [Azilian](/wiki/Azilian "Azilian") Culture. [Knut Stjerna](/wiki/Knut_Stjerna "Knut Stjerna") offered an alternative in the "Epipaleolithic", suggesting a final phase of the Paleolithic rather than an intermediate age in its own right inserted between the Paleolithic and Neolithic.

By the time of [Vere Gordon Childe](/wiki/Vere_Gordon_Childe "Vere Gordon Childe")'s work, *The Dawn of Europe* (1947), which affirms the Mesolithic, sufficient data had been collected to determine that a transitional period between the Paleolithic and the Neolithic was indeed a useful concept.[[2]](#cite_note-2) However, the terms "Mesolithic" and "Epipalaeolithic" remain in competition, with varying conventions of usage. In the archaeology of Northern Europe, for example for archaeological sites in Great Britain, Germany, Scandinavia, Ukraine, and Russia, the term "Mesolithic" is almost always used. In the archaeology of other areas, the term "Epipaleolithic" may be preferred by most authors, or there may be divergences between authors over which term to use or what meaning to assign to each. In the New World, neither term is used (except provisionally in the Arctic).

"Epipaleolithic" is sometimes also used alongside "Mesolithic" for the final end of the Upper Paleolithic immediately followed by the Mesolithic.[[3]](#cite_note-3) As "Mesolithic" suggests an intermediate period, followed by the Neolithic, some authors prefer the term "Epipaleolithic" for [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") cultures who are not succeeded by agricultural traditions, reserving "Mesolithic" for cultures who are clearly succeeded by the Neolithic Revolution, such as the [Natufian culture](/wiki/Natufian_culture "Natufian culture"). Other authors use "Mesolithic" as a generic term for hunter-gatherer cultures after the Last Glacial Maximum, whether they are transitional towards agriculture or not. In addition, terminology appears to differ between archaeological sub-disciplines, with "Mesolithic" being widely used in European archaeology, while "Epipalaeolithic" is more common in Near Eastern archaeology.

## Europe

Further information: [Prehistoric Europe § Mesolithic (Middle Stone Age)](/wiki/Prehistoric_Europe#Mesolithic_(Middle_Stone_Age) "Prehistoric Europe")

The [Balkan Mesolithic](/wiki/Balkan_Mesolithic "Balkan Mesolithic") begins around 15,000 years ago. In Western Europe, the Early Mesolithic, or [Azilian](/wiki/Azilian "Azilian"), begins about 14,000 years ago, in the [Franco-Cantabrian region](/wiki/Franco-Cantabrian_region "Franco-Cantabrian region") of northern [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [Southern France](/wiki/Southern_France "Southern France"). In other parts of Europe, the Mesolithic begins by 11,500 years ago (the beginning of the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene")), and it ends with the [introduction](/wiki/Neolithic_Europe "Neolithic Europe") of farming, depending on the region between c. 8,500 and 5,500 years ago. Regions that experienced greater environmental effects as the [last glacial period](/wiki/Last_glacial_period "Last glacial period") ended have a much more apparent Mesolithic era, lasting millennia.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In northern Europe, for example, societies were able to live well on rich food supplies from the marshlands created by the warmer climate. Such conditions produced distinctive human behaviors that are preserved in the material record, such as the [Maglemosian](/wiki/Maglemosian "Maglemosian") and [Azilian](/wiki/Azilian "Azilian") cultures. Such conditions also delayed the coming of the Neolithic until some 5,500 BP in northern Europe.

The type of stone toolkit remains one of the most diagnostic features: the Mesolithic used a [microlithic technology](/wiki/Microlithic_technology "Microlithic technology") – composite devices manufactured with Mode V chipped [stone tools](/wiki/Stone_tool "Stone tool") ([microliths](/wiki/Microliths "Microliths")), while the Paleolithic had utilized Modes I–IV. In some areas, however, such as Ireland, parts of Portugal, the Isle of Man and the Tyrrhenian Islands, a macrolithic technology was used in the Mesolithic.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In the Neolithic, the microlithic technology was replaced by a macrolithic technology, with an increased use of polished stone tools such as stone axes.

There is some evidence for the beginning of construction at sites with a ritual or [astronomical](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") significance, including [Stonehenge](/wiki/Stonehenge "Stonehenge"), with a short row of large [post holes](/wiki/Post_hole "Post hole") aligned east–west, and a possible "lunar calendar" at [Warren Field](/wiki/Warren_Field "Warren Field") in Scotland, with pits of post holes of varying sizes, thought to reflect the [lunar phases](/wiki/Lunar_phase "Lunar phase"). Both are dated to before c. 9,000 BP (the 8th millennium BC).[[6]](#cite_note-6)

An ancient chewed gum made from the pitch of birch bark revealed that a woman enjoyed a meal of hazelnuts and duck about 5,700 years ago in southern Denmark.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Mesolithic people influenced Europe's forests by bringing favored plants like hazel with them.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

As the "[Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") package" (including farming, herding, polished stone axes, [timber longhouses](/wiki/Neolithic_long_house "Neolithic long house") and pottery) spread into Europe, the Mesolithic way of life was marginalized and eventually disappeared. Mesolithic adaptations such as sedentism, population size and use of plant foods are cited as evidence of the transition to agriculture.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Other Mesolithic communities rejected the Neolithic package likely as a result of ideological reluctance, different worldviews and an active rejection of the sedentary-farming lifestyle.[[11]](#cite_note-Furholt-11) In one sample from the [Blätterhöhle](/w/index.php?title=Bl%C3%A4tterh%C3%B6hle&action=edit&redlink=1 "Blätterhöhle (page does not exist)") in [Hagen](/wiki/Hagen "Hagen"), it seems that the descendants of Mesolithic people maintained a foraging lifestyle for more than 2000 years after the arrival of farming societies in the area;[[12]](#cite_note-12) such societies may be called "[Subneolithic](/wiki/Subneolithic "Subneolithic")". For hunter-gatherer communities, long-term close contact and integration in existing farming communities facilitated the adoption of a farming lifestyle. The integration of these hunter-gatherers in farming communities was made possible by their socially open character towards new members.[[11]](#cite_note-Furholt-11) In north-Eastern Europe, the hunting and fishing lifestyle continued into the [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") period in regions less suited to agriculture, and in [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") no Mesolithic period may be accepted, with the locally preferred "Older Stone Age" moving into the "Younger Stone Age".[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Art

Compared to the preceding Upper Paleolithic and the following Neolithic, there is rather less surviving art from the Mesolithic. The [Rock art of the Iberian Mediterranean Basin](/wiki/Rock_art_of_the_Iberian_Mediterranean_Basin "Rock art of the Iberian Mediterranean Basin"), which probably spreads across from the Upper Paleolithic, is a widespread phenomenon, much less well known than the cave-paintings of the Upper Paleolithic, with which it makes an interesting contrast. The sites are now mostly cliff faces in the open air, and the subjects are now mostly human rather than animal, with large groups of small figures; there are 45 figures at [Roca dels Moros](/wiki/Roca_dels_Moros "Roca dels Moros"). Clothing is shown, and scenes of dancing, fighting, hunting and food-gathering. The figures are much smaller than the animals of Paleolithic art, and depicted much more schematically, though often in energetic poses.[[14]](#cite_note-14) A few small engraved [pendants](/wiki/Pendant "Pendant") with suspension holes and simple engraved designs are known, some from northern Europe in [amber](/wiki/Amber "Amber"), and one from [Star Carr](/wiki/Star_Carr "Star Carr") in Britain in [shale](/wiki/Shale "Shale").[[15]](#cite_note-15) The [Elk's Head of Huittinen](/wiki/Elk%27s_Head_of_Huittinen "Elk's Head of Huittinen") is a rare Mesolithic animal carving in [soapstone](/wiki/Soapstone "Soapstone") from [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland").

The rock art in the [Urals](/wiki/Urals "Urals") appears to show similar changes after the Paleolithic, and the wooden [Shigir Idol](/wiki/Shigir_Idol "Shigir Idol") is a rare survival of what may well have been a very common material for sculpture. It is a plank of [larch](/wiki/Larch "Larch") carved with geometric motifs, but topped with a human head. Now in fragments, it would have stood over five metres tall.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The [Ain Sakhri figurine](/wiki/Ain_Sakhri_figurine "Ain Sakhri figurine") from Palestine is a Natufian carving in [calcite](/wiki/Calcite "Calcite").

A total of 33 [antler frontlets](/wiki/Star_Carr_Frontlets "Star Carr Frontlets") have been discovered at Star Carr.[[17]](#cite_note-17) These are red deer skulls modified to be worn by humans. Modified frontlets have also been discovered at Bedburg-Königshoven, Hohen Viecheln, Plau, and Berlin-Biesdorf.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Weaving

Weaving techniques were deployed to create shoes and baskets, the latter being of fine construction and decorated with dyes. Examples have been found in [Cueva de los Murciélagos](/wiki/Cueva_de_los_Murci%C3%A9lagos "Cueva de los Murciélagos") in Southern Spain that in 2023 were dated to 9,500 years ago.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Ceramic Mesolithic

See also: [Subneolithic](/wiki/Subneolithic "Subneolithic")

In North-Eastern [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"), and certain southern European and [North African](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") sites, a "ceramic Mesolithic" can be distinguished between c. 9,000 to 5,850 BP. Russian archaeologists prefer to describe such pottery-making cultures as Neolithic, even though farming is absent. This pottery-making Mesolithic culture can be found peripheral to the sedentary Neolithic cultures. It created a distinctive type of pottery, with point or knob base and flared rims, manufactured by methods not used by the Neolithic farmers. Though each area of Mesolithic ceramic developed an individual style, common features suggest a single point of origin.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The earliest manifestation of this type of pottery may be in the region around [Lake Baikal](/wiki/Lake_Baikal "Lake Baikal") in Siberia. It appears in the [Yelshanka culture](/wiki/Yelshanka_culture "Yelshanka culture") on the [Volga](/wiki/Volga "Volga") in Russia 9,000 years ago,[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) and from there spread via the [Dnieper-Donets culture](/wiki/Dnieper-Donets_culture "Dnieper-Donets culture") to the [Narva culture](/wiki/Narva_culture "Narva culture") of the Eastern Baltic. Spreading westward along the coastline it is found in the [Ertebølle culture](/wiki/Erteb%C3%B8lle_culture "Ertebølle culture") of [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and Ellerbek of Northern Germany, and the related [Swifterbant culture](/wiki/Swifterbant_culture "Swifterbant culture") of the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/National_Museum_of_China_2014.02.01_14-43-38.jpg/250px-National_Museum_of_China_2014.02.01_14-43-38.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Museum_of_China_2014.02.01_14-43-38.jpg)

Pottery with re-construction repairs found in [Xianrendong](/wiki/Xianrendong "Xianrendong") cave, dating to 20,000–10,000 years ago.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

A 2012 publication in the journal *[Science](/wiki/Science_(journal) "Science (journal)")* announced that the earliest pottery yet known anywhere in the world was found in Xianrendong cave in China, dating by radiocarbon to between 20,000 and 19,000 years before present, at the end of the [Last Glacial Period](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Xianrendong-29) The carbon-14 datation was established by carefully dating surrounding sediments.[[29]](#cite_note-Xianrendong-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Science-30) Many of the pottery fragments had scorch marks, suggesting that the pottery was used for cooking.[[30]](#cite_note-Science-30) These early pottery containers were made well before the [invention of agriculture](/wiki/Invention_of_agriculture "Invention of agriculture") (dated to 10,000 to 8,000 BC), by mobile foragers who hunted and gathered their food during the Late Glacial Maximum.[[30]](#cite_note-Science-30)

[Comb Ceramic culture](/wiki/Comb_Ceramic_culture "Comb Ceramic culture") existed from around 4200 BC to around 2000 BC. The bearers of the culture are thought to have still mostly followed the Mesolithic [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") lifestyle.

## Mesolithic outside of Europe

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Stone_Age_Stone_Mortar_%26_Pestle%2C_Kebaran_culture%2C_22000-18000_BP.jpg/250px-Stone_Age_Stone_Mortar_%26_Pestle%2C_Kebaran_culture%2C_22000-18000_BP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stone_Age_Stone_Mortar_%26_Pestle,_Kebaran_culture,_22000-18000_BP.jpg)

Mesolithic stone mortar and pestle, [Kebaran culture](/wiki/Kebaran_culture "Kebaran culture"), [Epipaleolithic Near East](/wiki/Epipaleolithic_Near_East "Epipaleolithic Near East"). 22,000–18,000 BP

While Paleolithic and Neolithic have been found useful terms and concepts in the [archaeology of China](/wiki/Archaeology_of_China "Archaeology of China"), and can be mostly regarded as happily naturalized, Mesolithic was introduced later, mostly after 1945, and does not appear to be a necessary or useful term in the context of China. Chinese sites that have been regarded as Mesolithic are better considered as Early Neolithic.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In the [archaeology of India](/wiki/Archaeology_of_India "Archaeology of India"), the Mesolithic, dated roughly between 12,000 and 8,000 BP, remains a concept in use.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In the [archaeology of the Americas](/wiki/Archaeology_of_the_Americas "Archaeology of the Americas"), an [Archaic](/wiki/Archaic_period_(North_America) "Archaic period (North America)") or Meso-Indian period, following the [Lithic stage](/wiki/Lithic_stage "Lithic stage"), somewhat equates to the Mesolithic.

The [Saharan rock paintings](/wiki/Saharan_rock_art "Saharan rock art") found at [Tassili n'Ajjer](/wiki/Tassili_n%27Ajjer "Tassili n'Ajjer") in central [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara"), and at other locations depict vivid scenes of everyday life in central [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"). Some of these paintings were executed by a hunting people who lived in a [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") region teeming with a water-dependent species like the [hippopotamus](/wiki/Hippopotamus "Hippopotamus"), animals that no longer exist in the now-desert area.[[36]](#cite_note-36)
